title: Qnity Electronics, Inc. (Q) Intrinsic Value
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of Qnity Electronics (Q) compiled entirely by Gemini 3.1.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y5)
fcfGrowth.rationale: A 7% growth rate anticipates steady demand for industrial automation components, supported by nearshoring trends, while accounting for cyclical fluctuations.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: An 8.0% discount rate reflects a solid balance sheet and predictable cash flows from long-term contracts, with a slight premium for industrial cyclicality risk.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 2.5% terminal growth rate assumes the company will grow slightly faster than general inflation, driven by the perpetual need for technological upgrades in manufacturing.

thesisParagraphs: Qnity Electronics is the type of underlying infrastructure company that rarely makes headlines but consistently generates robust free cash flows. By embedding its proprietary electronic components into complex industrial machinery and automated assembly lines, it creates a business model characterized by extremely high switching costs. Once engineered into a system, clients rarely swap out Qnity components to save a few dollars.

Currently, the market appears to be undervaluing Qnity's resilience and its leverage to the ongoing mega-trend of supply chain nearshoring and factory automation. With nearly $800 million in annual free cash flow and a clear pathway to steady, mid-single-digit growth, the stock presents a compelling margin of safety at current valuations.
verdictFooter: Based on FY2025 actuals. Data via S&P Global (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: Why is Qnity considered undervalued?
faq.answer: The current market price does not fully reflect the company's strong free cash flow generation and its highly defensible position within the industrial automation supply chain.

faq.question: What drives Qnity's economic moat?
faq.answer: Its moat is primarily driven by high switching costs. Redesigning industrial systems to accommodate different electronic components is risky and expensive for clients.

faq.question: What is the biggest risk to the DCF model?
faq.answer: A severe, prolonged industrial recession that causes manufacturers to halt capital expenditures and delay automation upgrades.
